subject: ap-government
title: SCOTUS Comparison — Bush v. Vera (1996)
stimulus:
As a result of the 1990 census, Texas was given three additional congressional districts. In the redistricting process that followed, the Texas state legislature redrew the borders of its current districts to make room for the three new ones. Texas voter Al Vera sued the state of Texas for creating gerrymandered districts. In response, the state of Texas cited compliance with federal regulation of federal elections, as well as seeking to reduce the incumbency advantage.

Supporters of the plan claimed that the districts establish minority-majority districts that would ensure minority representation in Congress. The Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit struck parts of the plan, and the state of Texas appealed to the Supreme Court.

In Bush v. Vera, the Court held in favor of Vera and struck down the redistricting plan. Justice Sandra Day O'Connor reasoned that race was the primary factor used to redraw the districts and that other, race-neutral factors were given less consideration. The Court used the highest standard of review to determine the constitutionality of the government discrimination on the basis of race.

parts.prompt: Identify the constitutional clause that is the basis for the decisions in both Shaw v. Reno (1993) and Bush v. Vera (1996).
parts.scoring_notes: Accept 'Equal Protection Clause' or 'Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.' Only the Equal Protection Clause earns the point.

scoring_points.description: Identify the constitutional clause that is the basis for the decisions in both Shaw v. Reno (1993) and Bush v. Vera (1996).

required_elements: 1 pt: Identifies the Equal Protection Clause (of the Fourteenth Amendment) as the constitutional basis for both decisions.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Identifies the Equal Protection Clause (of the Fourteenth Amendment) as the constitutional basis for both decisions.

scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for identifying the constitutional clause that is the basis for the decisions in both Shaw v. Reno (1993) and Bush v. Vera (1996). Acceptable responses: Equal Protection Clause; Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.

sample_responses.response: The Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct identification — Equal Protection Clause is the constitutional basis for both Shaw v. Reno and Bush v. Vera. Earns the point.
CB accepts this shorter form. Earns the point.
Incorrect clause — Commerce Clause deals with interstate commerce, not redistricting or equal protection. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how the facts in Shaw v. Reno and Bush v. Vera led to similar holdings.
parts.scoring_notes: First point requires accurate description of Shaw v. Reno. Second point requires connecting both cases through the common thread of race being used as the primary redistricting factor and the Equal Protection Clause violation.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in Shaw v. Reno and Bush v. Vera led to similar holdings. (1 point — describe relevant fact/holding about the required Supreme Court case)

required_elements: 1 pt (first point): Describes relevant information about the required Supreme Court case Shaw v. Reno. Accept: in Shaw v. Reno, congressional reapportionment in North Carolina created a majority-minority district; the Supreme Court held in Shaw that a majority-minority district could be challenged if race was the only factor used in creating that district.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt (first point): Describes relevant information about the required Supreme Court case Shaw v. Reno. Accept: in Shaw v. Reno, congressional reapportionment in North Carolina created a majority-minority district; the Supreme Court held in Shaw that a majority-minority district could be challenged if race was the only factor used in creating that district.

common_traps: Describing only one case without explaining the comparison does not earn the 2-point tier
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B — 1 point: Describes relevant information (facts or holding) about the required Supreme Court case Shaw v. Reno. Acceptable responses: 'In Shaw v. Reno, congressional reapportionment in North Carolina created a majority-minority district.' 'The Supreme Court held in Shaw that a majority-minority district could be challenged if race was the only factor used in creating that district.'

sample_responses.response: In Shaw v. Reno, North Carolina's congressional reapportionment created an oddly shaped majority-minority district, and the Supreme Court held that a district could be challenged if race was the only factor used in drawing it.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurate description of Shaw's facts and holding — race-based majority-minority district subject to challenge. Earns 1 point (tier 1).

sample_responses.response: In Shaw v. Reno, a North Carolina redistricting plan created an unusually shaped majority-minority district for the purpose of ensuring minority representation.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes a relevant fact about Shaw. Earns 1 point (tier 1).

sample_responses.response: Shaw v. Reno is an important case from 1993.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — no specific fact or holding described. 0 pts.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the facts in Shaw v. Reno and Bush v. Vera led to similar holdings. (2 points — correctly explains how facts of BOTH cases led to different/similar holdings)

required_elements: 2 pts (second point): Correctly explains how the facts of both cases led to similar holdings. Accept: both cases used race to determine how to draw congressional districts — in both cases, the Court held that using race as the primary factor in redistricting violated the Constitution; in Shaw, a state used race as a primary factor to draw a congressional district and the Court held this was unconstitutional; in Bush, Texas also used race as a primary factor to draw congressional districts and the Court similarly held these redistricting plans were not permissible.
alternatives.correct_example: 2 pts (second point): Correctly explains how the facts of both cases led to similar holdings. Accept: both cases used race to determine how to draw congressional districts — in both cases, the Court held that using race as the primary factor in redistricting violated the Constitution; in Shaw, a state used race as a primary factor to draw a congressional district and the Court held this was unconstitutional; in Bush, Texas also used race as a primary factor to draw congressional districts and the Court similarly held these redistricting plans were not permissible.

common_traps: Describing only one case earns 1 point, not 2
Must connect facts to different outcomes
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B — 2 points: Correctly explains how the facts in both cases led to similar holdings. Acceptable responses: 'Both cases used race to determine how to draw congressional districts. In both cases, the Court held that using race as the primary factor in redistricting violated the Constitution.' 'In Shaw, a state used race as a primary factor to draw a congressional district, and the Court held that this was not constitutional. In Bush, Texas also used race as a primary factor to draw congressional districts and the Court similarly held that these redistricting plans were not permissible.'

sample_responses.response: In Shaw v. Reno, North Carolina used race as the primary factor to draw a congressional district, and the Court held this was unconstitutional under the Equal Protection Clause. Similarly, in Bush v. Vera, Texas used race as the primary factor to draw congressional districts, and the Court also held these redistricting plans were not permissible. Both cases produced similar holdings because in both, race was the dominant factor while other race-neutral factors received less consideration.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains the facts and holdings of BOTH cases and connects them through the common thread of race as the primary factor triggering equal-protection violations. Earns 2 points.

sample_responses.response: Both cases used race to draw congressional districts and the Court held that using race as the primary factor in redistricting violated the Constitution.
sample_responses.commentary: Brief but sufficient — connects facts of both cases to the same holding. Earns 2 points.

sample_responses.response: Shaw v. Reno dealt with a North Carolina redistricting plan.
sample_responses.commentary: Only describes one case. Does not earn the 2-point tier — caps at 1 point under b1 instead. 0 pts for b2.

parts.prompt: Explain how the decision in Bush v. Vera relates to the democratic ideal of republicanism.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain the connection between the Bush ruling and the democratic ideal of republicanism — the principle that elected officials should represent constituents fairly, and that electoral systems should allow citizens to choose their representatives.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the decision in Bush v. Vera relates to the democratic ideal of republicanism.

required_elements: 1 pt: Explains the connection between Bush v. Vera and republicanism. Accept: the decision in Bush emphasized that representation, not race, should be the primary focus of congressional redistricting — this reflects the ideal of republicanism in that voters should have a fair opportunity to select who represents them in government; the republican ideal supports representing the people's will through elected officials, and by insisting all constituencies should have an equal chance at representation, the decision in Bush showed the Court supported a redistricting plan that would best represent the voices of all Texans.
alternatives.correct_example: 1 pt: Explains the connection between Bush v. Vera and republicanism. Accept: the decision in Bush emphasized that representation, not race, should be the primary focus of congressional redistricting — this reflects the ideal of republicanism in that voters should have a fair opportunity to select who represents them in government; the republican ideal supports representing the people's will through elected officials, and by insisting all constituencies should have an equal chance at representation, the decision in Bush showed the Court supported a redistricting plan that would best represent the voices of all Texans.

scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the decision in Bush v. Vera relates to the democratic ideal of republicanism. Acceptable explanations: the decision in Bush emphasized that representation, not race, should be the primary focus of congressional redistricting — this reflects the ideal of republicanism in that voters should have a fair opportunity to select who represents them in government; the republican ideal is the support for representing the people's will in the government through elected officials — by insisting that all constituencies should have an equal chance at representation in Congress, the decision in Bush shows that the Court supported a redistricting plan that would best represent the voices of all Texans.

sample_responses.response: The decision in Bush v. Vera reflects the democratic ideal of republicanism because republicanism holds that the people's will should be represented in government through elected officials. By striking down the race-dominated redistricting plan, the Court insisted that all constituencies should have an equal chance at meaningful representation — ensuring that Texas's congressional districts would best reflect the voices of all Texans rather than being drawn primarily along racial lines.
sample_responses.commentary: Defines republicanism and explains how Bush v. Vera's insistence on fair representation operationalizes that ideal. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Republicanism is the ideal that voters choose representatives who act on their behalf. The Bush v. Vera decision supported this ideal by emphasizing that representation, not race, should be the primary focus of congressional redistricting — so voters get a fair opportunity to select who represents them.
sample_responses.commentary: Connects the Court's emphasis on representation over race to the republican ideal of elected representation. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Bush v. Vera is a Supreme Court case about redistricting.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not explain how the decision relates to republicanism — simply describes the case. 0 pts.
